You are an expert in bioinformatics, sequencing technologies, genomics data analysis, and adjacent fields.

You are given findings from a MultiQC report, generated by a bioinformatics workflow.
MultiQC supports various bioinformatics tools that output QC metrics, and aggregates those metrics
into a single report. It outputs a "General Statistics" table with key metrics for each sample across
all tools. That table is followed by more detailed sections from specific tools, that can include tables,
as well as plots of different types (bar plot, line plot, scatter plot, heatmap, etc.)

You are given data from such a report. Your task is to analyse the data, and
give 1-2 bullet points of a very short and concise overall summary for the results.
Don't waste words: mention only the important QC issues. If there are no issues, just say so.
Just print one or two bullet points, nothing else.
Please do not add any extra headers to the response.

Use markdown to format your reponse for readability. Use directives with pre-defined classes
.text-green, .text-red, and .text-yellow to highlight severity, e.g. :span[39.2%]{.text-red}.
Highlight any mentioned sample names or sample named prefixes or suffixes with a sample directive,
and make sure to use the same color classes for severity, e.g. :sample[A1001.2003]{.text-yellow}
or :sample[A1001]{.text-yellow}. Do not put multiple sample names inside one directive.

You must use only multiples of 4 spaces to indent nested lists.



Two examples of short summaries:

- :span[11/13 samples]{.text-green} show consistent metrics within expected ranges.
- :sample[A1001.2003]{.text-red} and :sample[A1001.2004]{.text-red} exhibit extremely high percentage of :span[duplicates]{.text-red} (:span[65.54%]{.text-red} and :span[83.14%]{.text-red}, respectively).

- All samples show good quality metrics with :span[75.7-77.0%]{.text-green} CpG methylation and :span[76.3-86.0%]{.text-green} alignment rates
- :sample[2wk]{.text-yellow} samples show slightly higher duplication (:span[11-15%]{.text-yellow}) compared to :sample[1wk]{.text-green} samples (:span[6-9%]{.text-green})'



----------------------

Tools used in the report:

1. Samtools Flagstat
Description: <p>Toolkit for interacting with BAM/CRAM files.</p>
Links: http://www.htslib.org

----------------------

2. Mosdepth
Description: <p>Fast BAM/CRAM depth calculation for WGS, exome, or targeted sequencing.</p>
Links: https://github.com/brentp/mosdepth

----------------------

3. Bcftools
Description: <p>Utilities for variant calling and manipulating VCFs and BCFs.</p>
Links: https://samtools.github.io/bcftools/

----------------------

4. Vcftools
Description: <p>Program to analyse and reporting on VCF files.</p>
Links: https://vcftools.github.io

----------------------

5. SNPeff
Description: <p>Annotates and predicts the effects of variants on genes (such as amino acid changes).</p>
Links: http://snpeff.sourceforge.net/

----------------------

6. VEP
Description: <p>Determines the effect of variants on genes, transcripts and protein sequences, as well as regulatory regions.</p>
Links: https://www.ensembl.org/info/docs/tools/vep/index.html

----------------------

7. nf-core/sarek Methods Description
Description: <p>Suggested text and references to use when describing pipeline usage within the methods section of a publication.</p>
Links: https://github.com/nf-core/sarek

----------------------

8. nf-core/sarek Workflow Summary
Description: <ul>
<li>this information is collected when the pipeline is started.</li>
</ul>
Links: https://github.com/nf-core/sarek

----------------------

----------------------

Tool: Samtools Flagstat
Section: Percent mapped
Section description: Alignment metrics from <code>samtools stats</code>; mapped vs. unmapped reads vs. reads mapped with MQ0.
Section help text: For a set of samples that have come from the same multiplexed library,
similar numbers of reads for each sample are expected. Large differences in numbers might
indicate issues during the library preparation process. Whilst large differences in read
numbers may be controlled for in downstream processings (e.g. read count normalisation),
you may wish to consider whether the read depths achieved have fallen below recommended
levels depending on the applications.
Low alignment rates could indicate contamination of samples (e.g. adapter sequences),
low sequencing quality or other artefacts. These can be further investigated in the
sequence level QC (e.g. from FastQC).
Reads mapped with MQ0 often indicate that the reads are ambiguously mapped to multiple
locations in the reference sequence. This can be due to repetitive regions in the genome,
the presence of alternative contigs in the reference, or due to reads that are too short
to be uniquely mapped. These reads are often filtered out in downstream analyses.
Title: Samtools: stats: Alignment Scores

Plot type: bar plot
Values: # Reads

|Sample|Mapped (with MQ>0)|MQ0|Unmapped|
|---|---|---|---|
|220884_LUL_HighSR.recal|63,984,619|1,752,451|61,090|
|220884_LB6_HighSR.recal|51,892,773|1,777,313|168,326|
|220754_RB6_LowSR.recal|61,720,294|2,005,107|172,957|
|220742_LUL_HighSR.recal|48,593,566|1,490,948|145,039|
|220713_RB6_LowSR.recal|60,457,903|1,810,603|131,537|
|220597_MCC_LowSR.recal|56,478,669|1,643,607|89,186|
|220578_LUL_LowSR.recal|62,154,573|1,801,933|136,700|
|220514_LUL_HighSR.recal|58,332,709|1,697,135|93,936|
|217788_LB89_LowSR.recal|56,254,699|1,923,168|207,555|
|108159_LUL_LowSR.recal|65,965,334|2,351,733|217,889|

Tool: Samtools Flagstat
Section: Alignment stats
Section description: This module parses the output from <code>samtools stats</code>. All numbers in millions.
Title: Samtools: stats: Alignment Stats

Plot type: violin plot

Number of samples: 10

Metrics:
Total sequences - Total sequences
Mapped &amp; paired - Paired-end technology bit set + both mates mapped
Properly paired - Proper-pair bit set
Duplicated - PCR or optical duplicate bit set
QC Failed - QC Failed
Reads MQ0 - Reads mapped and MQ=0
Mapped bases (CIGAR) - Mapped bases (CIGAR)
Bases Trimmed - Bases Trimmed
Duplicated bases - Duplicated bases
Diff chromosomes - Pairs on different chromosomes
Other orientation - Pairs with other orientation
Inward pairs - Inward oriented pairs
Outward pairs - Outward oriented pairs

|Sample Name|Total sequences|Mapped &amp; paired|Properly paired|Duplicated|QC Failed|Reads MQ0|Mapped bases (CIGAR)|Bases Trimmed|Duplicated bases|Diff chromosomes|Other orientation|Inward pairs|Outward pairs|
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
|108159_LUL_LowSR.recal|68.53|68.10|67.47|12.87|0.0|2.35|9474.80|0.0|1858.74|0.19|0.01|28.19|5.67|

|220884_LUL_HighSR.recal|65.80|65.68|64.96|9.48|0.0|1.75|9472.68|0.0|1374.89|0.25|0.01|27.84|4.74|

----------------------

Tool: Mosdepth
Section: Cumulative coverage distribution
Section description: Proportion of bases in the reference genome with, at least, a given depth of coverage. Note that for 10 samples, a BED file was provided, so the data was calculated across those regions. For 10 samples, it's calculated across the entire genome length. 10 samples have both global and region reports, and we are showing the data for regions
Section help text: For a set of DNA or RNA reads mapped to a reference sequence, such as a genome
or transcriptome, the depth of coverage at a given base position is the number
of high-quality reads that map to the reference at that position, while the
breadth of coverage is the fraction of the reference sequence to which reads
have been mapped with at least a given depth of coverage
(<a href="https://doi.org/10.1038/nrg3642" target="_blank">Sims et al. 2014</a>).
Defining coverage breadth in terms of coverage depth is useful, because
sequencing experiments typically require a specific minimum depth of coverage
over the region of interest (<a href="https://doi.org/10.1038/nrg3642"
target="_blank">Sims et al. 2014</a>), so the extent of the reference sequence
that is amenable to analysis is constrained to lie within regions that have
sufficient depth. With inadequate sequencing breadth, it can be difficult to
distinguish the absence of a biological feature (such as a gene) from a lack
of data (<a href="https://doi.org/10.1101/gr.7050807" target="_blank">Green 2007</a>).
For increasing coverage depths (<em>1&#215;, 2&#215;, &#8230;, N&#215;</em>),
coverage breadth is calculated as the percentage of the reference
sequence that is covered by at least that number of reads, then plots
coverage breadth (y-axis) against coverage depth (x-axis). This plot
shows the relationship between sequencing depth and breadth for each read
dataset, which can be used to gauge, for example, the likely effect of a
minimum depth filter on the fraction of a genome available for analysis.
Title: Mosdepth: Cumulative coverage distribution

Plot type: x/y line
X axis: Cumulative Coverage (X)
Y axis: % bases in genome/regions covered by at least X reads

Samples: 108159_LUL_LowSR.recal, 217788_LB89_LowSR.recal, 220514_LUL_HighSR.recal, 220578_LUL_LowSR.recal, 220597_MCC_LowSR.recal, 220713_RB6_LowSR.recal, 220742_LUL_HighSR.recal, 220754_RB6_LowSR.recal, 220884_LB6_HighSR.recal, 220884_LUL_HighSR.recal

Y values are in %

X values are in X

Tool: Vcftools
Section: TsTv by Count
Section description: Plot of <code>TSTV-BY-COUNT</code> - the transition to transversion ratio as a function of alternative allele count from the output of vcftools TsTv-by-count.
Section help text: <code>Transition</code> is a purine-to-purine or pyrimidine-to-pyrimidine point mutations.
<code>Transversion</code> is a purine-to-pyrimidine or pyrimidine-to-purine point mutation.
<code>Alternative allele count</code> is the number of alternative alleles at the site.
Note: only bi-allelic SNPs are used (multi-allelic sites and INDELs are skipped.)
Refer to Vcftools's manual (https://vcftools.github.io/man_latest.html) on <code>--TsTv-by-count</code>
Title: VCFTools: TsTv by Count

Plot type: x/y line
X axis: Alternative Allele Count
Y axis: TsTv Ratio

Samples: 108159_LUL_LowSR.mutect2.filtered.TsTv.count, 217788_LB89_LowSR.mutect2.filtered.TsTv.count, 220514_LUL_HighSR.mutect2.filtered.TsTv.count, 220578_LUL_LowSR.mutect2.filtered.TsTv.count, 220597_MCC_LowSR.mutect2.filtered.TsTv.count, 220713_RB6_LowSR.mutect2.filtered.TsTv.count, 220742_LUL_HighSR.mutect2.filtered.TsTv.count, 220754_RB6_LowSR.mutect2.filtered.TsTv.count, 220884_LB6_HighSR.mutect2.filtered.TsTv.count, 220884_LUL_HighSR.mutect2.filtered.TsTv.count

Tool: Vcftools
Section: TsTv by Qual
Section description: Plot of <code>TSTV-BY-QUAL</code> - the transition to transversion ratio as a function of SNP quality from the output of vcftools TsTv-by-qual.
Section help text: <code>Transition</code> is a purine-to-purine or pyrimidine-to-pyrimidine point mutations.
<code>Transversion</code> is a purine-to-pyrimidine or pyrimidine-to-purine point mutation.
<code>Quality</code> here is the Phred-scaled quality score as given in the QUAL column of VCF.
Note: only bi-allelic SNPs are used (multi-allelic sites and INDELs are skipped.)
Refer to Vcftools's manual (https://vcftools.github.io/man_latest.html) on <code>--TsTv-by-qual</code>

genomic regions is 5000bp by default.

Tool: SNPeff
Section: Variant Effects by Impact
Section description: The stacked bar plot shows the putative impact of detected
variants and the number of variants for each impact.
Section help text: There are four levels of impacts predicted by SnpEff:
<ul>
<li><strong>High</strong>: High impact (like stop codon)</li>
<li><strong>Moderate</strong>: Middle impact (like same type of amino acid substitution)</li>
<li><strong>Low</strong>: Low impact (ie silence mutation)</li>
<li><strong>Modifier</strong>: No impact</li>
</ul>
Title: SnpEff: Counts by Effects Impact

Plot type: bar plot
Values: # Reads

|Sample|High|Moderate|Low|Modifier|
|---|---|---|---|---|
|220884_LUL_HighSR.mutect2.filtered_snpEff|5,583|47,125|209,393|252,169|
|220884_LB6_HighSR.mutect2.filtered_snpEff|25,085|154,878|602,922|765,231|

Tool: SNPeff
Section: Variants by Functional Class
Section description: The stacked bar plot shows the effect of variants and
the number of variants for each effect type.
Section help text: This plot shows the effect of variants on the translation of
the mRNA as protein. There are three possible cases:
<ul>
<li><strong>Silent</strong>: The amino acid does not change.</li>
<li><strong>Missense</strong>: The amino acid is different.</li>
<li><strong>Nonsense</strong>: The variant generates a stop codon.</li>
</ul>
Title: SnpEff: Counts by Functional Class

Plot type: bar plot
Values: # Reads

|Sample|Nonsense|Missense|Silent|
|---|---|---|---|
|220884_LUL_HighSR.mutect2.filtered_snpEff|183|34,240|196,957|
|220884_LB6_HighSR.mutect2.filtered_snpEff|541|108,652|562,508|

|108159_LUL_LowSR.mutect2.filtered_VEP.ann|1,777|1,080|2,190|30,757|736|224|3,095|4,085|190,075|734|1,649|36,552|7,089|28,627|33|351|608,090|21|148|17,242|34,567|31,737|140,869|922|241|6|201|

### Coding consequences

|Sample|stop_gained|frameshift_variant|stop_lost|start_lost|inframe_insertion|inframe_deletion|missense_variant|protein_altering_variant|incomplete_terminal_codon_variant|start_retained_variant|stop_retained_variant|synonymous_variant|coding_sequence_variant|
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|

----------------------

Tool: nf-core/sarek Methods Description
Section: 
<h4>Methods</h4>
<p>Data was processed using nf-core/sarek v3.8.1 (doi: <a href='https://doi.org/10.12688/f1000research.16665.2'>10.12688/f1000research.16665.2</a>), (doi: <a href='https://doi.org/10.1093/nargab/lqae031'>10.1093/nargab/lqae031</a>), (doi: <a href='https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.3476425'>10.5281/zenodo.3476425</a>) of the nf-core collection of workflows (<a href="https://doi.org/10.1038/s41587-020-0439-x">Ewels <em>et al.</em>, 2020</a>), utilising reproducible software environments from the Bioconda (<a href="https://doi.org/10.1038/s41592-018-0046-7">Grüning <em>et al.</em>, 2018</a>) and Biocontainers (<a href="https://doi.org/10.1093/bioinformatics/btx192">da Veiga Leprevost <em>et al.</em>, 2017</a>) projects.</p>
<p>The pipeline was executed with Nextflow v25.10.2 (<a href="https://doi.org/10.1038/nbt.3820">Di Tommaso <em>et al.</em>, 2017</a>) with the following command:</p>
<pre><code>nextflow run /projects/tdanhorn@xsede.org/bbsr/software/nf-core/nf-core-sarek-3.8.1/3_8_1 -profile curc_alpine -ansi-log false --wes --input /scratch/alpine/aassante@xsede.org/alexis/analysis/exome_SR/recalibrated.csv --genome GATK.GRCh38 --igenomes_base /projects/aassante@xsede.org/references/igenomes --fasta /projects/aassante@xsede.org/references/igenomes/Homo_sapiens/GATK/GRCh38/Sequence/WholeGenomeFasta/Homo_sapiens_assembly38.fasta --step variant_calling --intervals /scratch/alpine/aassante@xsede.org/alexis/analysis/exome_SR/ptargets.bed --tools mutect2,vep,snpeff --outdir /scratch/alpine/aassante@xsede.org/alexis/analysis/exome_SR/sarekRUN_hu_wtargets/ --multiqc_title 381_WES_SR_multiqc_updatedwithtargets -resume</code></pre>
<p></p>
<h4>References</h4>
<ul>
  <li>Di Tommaso, P., Chatzou, M., Floden, E. W., Barja, P. P., Palumbo, E., & Notredame, C. (2017). Nextflow enables reproducible computational workflows. Nature Biotechnology, 35(4), 316-319. doi: <a href="https://doi.org/10.1038/nbt.3820">10.1038/nbt.3820</a></li>
  <li>Ewels, P. A., Peltzer, A., Fillinger, S., Patel, H., Alneberg, J., Wilm, A., Garcia, M. U., Di Tommaso, P., & Nahnsen, S. (2020). The nf-core framework for community-curated bioinformatics pipelines. Nature Biotechnology, 38(3), 276-278. doi: <a href="https://doi.org/10.1038/s41587-020-0439-x">10.1038/s41587-020-0439-x</a></li>
  <li>Grüning, B., Dale, R., Sjödin, A., Chapman, B. A., Rowe, J., Tomkins-Tinch, C. H., Valieris, R., Köster, J., & Bioconda Team. (2018). Bioconda: sustainable and comprehensive software distribution for the life sciences. Nature Methods, 15(7), 475–476. doi: <a href="https://doi.org/10.1038/s41592-018-0046-7">10.1038/s41592-018-0046-7</a></li>
  <li>da Veiga Leprevost, F., Grüning, B. A., Alves Aflitos, S., Röst, H. L., Uszkoreit, J., Barsnes, H., Vaudel, M., Moreno, P., Gatto, L., Weber, J., Bai, M., Jimenez, R. C., Sachsenberg, T., Pfeuffer, J., Vera Alvarez, R., Griss, J., Nesvizhskii, A. I., & Perez-Riverol, Y. (2017). BioContainers: an open-source and community-driven framework for software standardization. Bioinformatics (Oxford, England), 33(16), 2580–2582. doi: <a href="https://doi.org/10.1093/bioinformatics/btx192">10.1093/bioinformatics/btx192</a></li>
  
</ul>
<div class="alert alert-info">
  <h5>Notes:</h5>
  <ul>
    
    <li>The command above does not include parameters contained in any configs or profiles that may have been used. Ensure the config file is also uploaded with your publication!</li>
    <li>You should also cite all software used within this run. Check the "Software Versions" of this report to get version information.</li>
  </ul>
</div>


----------------------

Tool: nf-core/sarek Workflow Summary
Section: 
<p style="font-size:110%"><b>Input/output options</b></p>
<dl class="dl-horizontal">
    <dt>input</dt><dd><samp>/scratch/alpine/aassante@xsede.org/alexis/analysis/exome_SR/recalibrated.csv</samp></dd>
    <dt>outdir</dt><dd><samp>/scratch/alpine/aassante@xsede.org/alexis/analysis/exome_SR/sarekRUN_hu_wtargets/</samp></dd>
    <dt>step</dt><dd><samp>variant_calling</samp></dd>
</dl>
<p style="font-size:110%"><b>Main options</b></p>
<dl class="dl-horizontal">
    <dt>intervals</dt><dd><samp>/scratch/alpine/aassante@xsede.org/alexis/analysis/exome_SR/ptargets.bed</samp></dd>
    <dt>tools</dt><dd><samp>mutect2,vep,snpeff</samp></dd>
    <dt>wes</dt><dd><samp>true</samp></dd>
</dl>
<p style="font-size:110%"><b>Variant Calling</b></p>
<dl class="dl-horizontal">
    <dt>cf_chrom_len</dt><dd><samp>/projects/aassante@xsede.org/references/igenomes/Homo_sapiens/GATK/GRCh38/Sequence/Length/Homo_sapiens_assembly38.len</samp></dd>
    <dt>pon</dt><dd><samp>/projects/aassante@xsede.org/references/igenomes/Homo_sapiens/GATK/GRCh38/Annotation/GATKBundle/1000g_pon.hg38.vcf.gz</samp></dd>
    <dt>pon_tbi</dt><dd><samp>/projects/aassante@xsede.org/references/igenomes/Homo_sapiens/GATK/GRCh38/Annotation/GATKBundle/1000g_pon.hg38.vcf.gz.tbi</samp></dd>
</dl>

    <dt>igenomes_base</dt><dd><samp>/projects/aassante@xsede.org/references/igenomes</samp></dd>
</dl>

    <dt>ascat_alleles</dt><dd><samp>/projects/aassante@xsede.org/references/igenomes/Homo_sapiens/GATK/GRCh38/Annotation/ASCAT/G1000_alleles_hg38.zip</samp></dd>
    <dt>ascat_genome</dt><dd><samp>hg38</samp></dd>
    <dt>ascat_loci</dt><dd><samp>/projects/aassante@xsede.org/references/igenomes/Homo_sapiens/GATK/GRCh38/Annotation/ASCAT/G1000_loci_hg38.zip</samp></dd>
    <dt>ascat_loci_gc</dt><dd><samp>/projects/aassante@xsede.org/references/igenomes/Homo_sapiens/GATK/GRCh38/Annotation/ASCAT/GC_G1000_hg38.zip</samp></dd>
    <dt>ascat_loci_rt</dt><dd><samp>/projects/aassante@xsede.org/references/igenomes/Homo_sapiens/GATK/GRCh38/Annotation/ASCAT/RT_G1000_hg38.zip</samp></dd>
    <dt>bwa</dt><dd><samp>/projects/aassante@xsede.org/references/igenomes/Homo_sapiens/GATK/GRCh38/Sequence/BWAIndex/</samp></dd>
    <dt>bwamem2</dt><dd><samp>/projects/aassante@xsede.org/references/igenomes/Homo_sapiens/GATK/GRCh38/Sequence/BWAmem2Index/</samp></dd>
    <dt>chr_dir</dt><dd><samp>/projects/aassante@xsede.org/references/igenomes/Homo_sapiens/GATK/GRCh38/Sequence/Chromosomes</samp></dd>

    <dt>dbsnp_tbi</dt><dd><samp>/projects/aassante@xsede.org/references/igenomes/Homo_sapiens/GATK/GRCh38/Annotation/GATKBundle/dbsnp_146.hg38.vcf.gz.tbi</samp></dd>
    <dt>dbsnp_vqsr</dt><dd><samp>--resource:dbsnp,known=false,training=true,truth=false,prior=2.0 dbsnp_146.hg38.vcf.gz</samp></dd>
    <dt>dict</dt><dd><samp>/projects/aassante@xsede.org/references/igenomes/Homo_sapiens/GATK/GRCh38/Sequence/WholeGenomeFasta/Homo_sapiens_assembly38.dict</samp></dd>
    <dt>dragmap</dt><dd><samp>/projects/aassante@xsede.org/references/igenomes/Homo_sapiens/GATK/GRCh38/Sequence/dragmap/</samp></dd>
